title: Closed Reeb Orbits on the Sphere and Symplectically Degenerate Maxima
speaker: Viktor Ginzburg

proof_stages.label: Two-orbit theorem and overall strategy
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem is that any contact form on S^3 inducing the standard contact structure has at least two closed Reeb orbits, and the proposed proof adapts Conley-conjecture ideas with only one genuinely three-dimensional step.

proof_stages.label: Local contact homology versus equivariant local Floer homology
proof_stages.summary: For an isolated closed Reeb orbit whose iterates remain isolated, the local contact homology of the kth iterate is identified over Q, up to the standard grading shift, with the Z_k-equivariant local Floer homology of the kth Poincare return map.

proof_stages.label: Symplectically degenerate maxima and Hingston's lemma
proof_stages.summary: An SDM is defined as a totally degenerate fixed point with nonzero top-degree local Floer homology, and Hingston's lemma converts this homological condition into a Hamiltonian representative for which the point is an isolated flat maximum with zero Hamiltonian eigenvalues.

proof_stages.label: Unique orbit on S^3 forces an SDM
proof_stages.summary: Assuming there is only one closed Reeb orbit on S^3, global contact-homology information together with three-dimensional index analysis and a Hofer-Zehnder injection force the return map of that orbit to be an SDM.

proof_stages.label: Action-window criterion for infinitely many orbits
proof_stages.summary: For an SDM orbit x of action c on a standard contact sphere, it suffices to prove that filtered contact homology in the window (kc,kc+epsilon) is nonzero for every sufficiently small epsilon and all large k, since this produces Reeb orbits with actions just above the iterates and hence infinitely many orbits.

proof_stages.label: Hamiltonian sandwich template from the Conley conjecture
proof_stages.summary: The Hamiltonian proof sandwiches the SDM Hamiltonian between lower and upper standard bump Hamiltonians, computes a nonzero map between their filtered Floer homology groups, and factors that map through the Floer homology of the iterated original Hamiltonian.

proof_stages.label: Contact adaptation and nonvanishing transfer
proof_stages.summary: In the contact setting, analogous inner and outer contact forms give a filtered contact-homology map factoring through the original form; energy barriers make the relevant local pieces direct summands, and the local contact-Floer comparison imports the Hamiltonian nonvanishing, proving the action-window theorem.
